manifest.title: UK tax and benefit reform timeline, 1986–2026
manifest.description: A rapid, indicative overview of the distributional direction of selected headline reforms across UK governments.

charts.title: Indicative reform-package counts by government period
charts.subtitle: Selected headline packages, 1986–2026; editorial classification, not an official series
charts.question: How does the estimated distributional direction of selected major reforms vary by government period?
charts.rationale: Grouped horizontal bars compare two heuristic counts across ten named periods without implying a continuous measured series.

blocks.body: # UK tax and benefit reform timeline, 1986–2026

- **The broad direction changes clearly with government.** The late-1980s and 1990s Conservative periods lean non-progressive; Labour after 1997 leans progressive; austerity after 2010 reverses much of that pattern.
- **The counts are deliberately approximate.** They count selected headline policy packages—not Acts, Budget measures or every parameter change—and classify mixed reforms by their likely first-order distributional effect.
- **Recent periods are especially mixed.** Pandemic and energy support pulled the Johnson and Sunak periods in a more progressive direction, while frozen thresholds and retained welfare restrictions pushed the other way.

## Four decades on one serpentine path
Read the landscape timeline left to right across the first row, follow the rounded turn, then continue right to left and back again. Each circular node opens an arrow-connected government card.

United Kingdom · 1986–2026

Tax & benefit reform timeline

Follow the path left → right, then back, then left → right. Select a year node for details.

Council Tax replaced poll tax, but VAT rose and unemployment and incapacity support tightened.

Lean · non-progressive

Lean · non-progressive

Pandemic support and a lower UC taper protected incomes; uplift withdrawal and frozen thresholds offset them.

Energy support protects incomes; the mini-budget, fiscal drag and NIC cuts tilt gains upward.

● SELECT A CIRCLE TO OPEN ITS CONNECTED CARD
Indicative, not official. Editorial counts of selected headline packages; mixed reforms are classified by likely dominant first-order distributional effect.
← Scroll landscape timeline →

## The strongest pattern is the 1997 and 2010 turning points
The selected packages suggest two large shifts: Labour’s post-1997 expansion of tax credits and pensioner support, then the post-2010 move toward working-age welfare restraint. This is more useful as a map of direction than as a league table: the fiscal size and durability of reforms vary widely, and one large policy can matter more than several small ones.

## Estimated reform direction by period
The bar comparison makes the editorial classification easier to scan, but it should not be read as a fiscal-impact measure. Counts give a small policy package the same visual weight as a large one, so direction matters more than the total.

## Best next step
Use this schematic as an orientation layer. For publication or quantitative comparison, replace the editorial counts with a reproducible policy inventory and distributional estimates from IFS, HM Treasury, DWP and HMRC.

## Questions a deeper version should answer
- Should temporary crisis support count equally with permanent structural reform?
- Should tax and benefit measures be weighted by fiscal cost or household income effect?
- Should reforms be classified by immediate cash impact, lifetime effect, or distribution across income deciles?

## Caveats and assumptions
The 40-year window begins in 1986. Prime-minister transitions are consolidated where the underlying policy direction is similar, except where a short administration created a distinct package. The 2024–2026 period includes announced and legislated measures whose full effects are not yet observable. Counts should not be summed into a precise historical score.

query.sql: SELECT * FROM (VALUES ('1986–90 Thatcher','Conservative',2,4),('1990–97 Major','Conservative',2,5),('1997–2001 Blair I','Labour',6,2),('2001–05 Blair II','Labour',6,2),('2005–10 Blair/Brown','Labour',8,4),('2010–15 Coalition','Coalition',4,8),('2015–19 Cameron/May','Conservative',5,9),('2019–22 Johnson','Conservative',6,4),('2022–24 Truss/Sunak','Conservative',6,9),('2024–26 Starmer','Labour',8,4)) AS t(period,party,progressive,non_progressive);
query.description: Manual classification of selected headline tax and benefit reform packages by likely first-order distributional direction.
filters: Selected headline tax and benefit packages only
metric_definitions: Progressive estimate: package likely to raise lower-income disposable income proportionally more or shift tax burden upward.
Non-progressive estimate: package whose dominant first-order distributional effect runs the other way.
